---
title: "`multinomialTS`: a state-space approach for modelling community time series"
author: "Quinn Asena • Jack Williams • Tony Ives"
date: today
bibliography: ../refs.bib
from: markdown+emoji
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    css: custom.css
    slide-number: true
    incremental: false
    embed-resources: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    logo: ./figures/dark-mode.png

title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-color: "#054746ff"
---


## Welcome {.smaller}

- Here, I will show two contrasting case studies to demonstrate how to fit `multinomialTS` to data with different characteristics.

- This will be followed by a coding demo for the programmatic workflow.


## People {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
- Jack Williams
- Tony Ives
- Angie Perotti
- Nora Schlenker
:::
::: {.column}
- David Nelson
- Bryan Schuman
- Jonathan Johnson
- Vania Stefanova
:::
::::

![](./figures/people.png){fig-align="center" width="700" fig-alt="Group photo of the project collaborators"}


## The aim of `multinomialTS` {.smaller}

- Disentangle the relative effects of abiotic drivers and biotic interactions
- Lend statistical support to hypotheses about the drivers of community change
- Tackle the statistical challenges of unevenly spaced time series and relative-abundance data (multinomially distributed data)


## The multinomial distribution {.smaller}

- Counts of $n$ individuals across $k$ categories, like rolling a
  **loaded** $k$-sided die $n$ times
- The counts must sum to $n$, so we can only estimate $k-1$ taxa

![Here is a stock image of dice! It's probably AI-generated...](./figures/dice.jpg){fig-align="center" width="45%" fig-alt="A stack of dice representing a multinomial distribution."}

## State-space modelling - what it estimates {.smaller}

_State-space modelling goes beyond descriptive approaches and attempts to estimate:_

<br>

- Autoregressive / density-dependent processes
- Interspecific interactions
- Driver-taxa relationships
- Combinations of the above

## State-space modelling - how it works {.smaller}

State-space modelling attempts to predict the "true" unobservable state of a system from observable variables. It does so via two equations:

<br>
Process equation:

$$
Z_t = B_0 + C(Z_{t-1} - (B_0 + BX_{t-1})) + BX_t + E_t
$$

$$
E_t \sim N(0, \sigma^2 V)
$$


Observation equation:

$$
Y_t = \text{Multinomial}(P_t, d)
$$

$$
\text{logit}(P_t) = Z_t
$$

$B_0$ = intercept, $C$ = interactions, $B$ = driver effects, $E_t$ = process error, $V$ = error covariance


## `multinomialTS` {.smaller}

- Models a multinomial distribution (i.e., count data) directly
- Accepts multiple covariates of mixed types
- Generates estimates of taxa interactions and driver-taxa relationships
- The full publication is available at [Asena _et al.,_ 2026](https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210x.70315)


## Fitting process {.smaller}

We will follow two case studies to demonstrate the decision process of fitting `multinomialTS`

- Decisions about the data
  - choosing focal taxa and reference group
  - choosing window-span/prediction resolution
- Decisions about the model
  - set up multiple working hypotheses [@chamberlin1897]
  - fit the model to hypotheses or components of each hypothesis

Each case study will follow the arc from setting up hypotheses, choosing focal taxa, and fitting the model.

## Case studies {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
#### Lake Tulane

- Florida, USA
- ~60,000 year-long record
- Covariates
  - fungal spores (proxy for megaherbivory)
  - $CO_2$ and $\delta^{18}O$ (climate)
  - Heinrich events (climate-related)
  - charcoal (fire)
- Williams _et al.,_ in review, @grimm1993; @grimm2006b
:::
::: {.column}

#### Sunfish Pond

- Pennsylvania, USA
- ~13,800 years
- Covariates
  - lake level (proxy for hydroclimate)
- Johnson _et al.,_ in review, includes more covariates to test local vs regional drivers
:::
::::


## Lake Tulane variables {.smaller}

![](./figures/tulane_esa.svg){fig-align="center" fig-alt="Lake Tulane record: time series of the selected pollen taxa alongside the climate, fire, and megaherbivory covariates over roughly 60,000 years"}

## Fitting Lake Tulane {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
#### Questions/hypotheses:

- Are biotic interactions or climatic variability the primary drivers of pine and oak dynamics?
- Is mega-herbivory a significant driver of vegetation change?
- Is fire a significant driver of vegetation change?
:::

::: {.column}
#### Parameter selection:

- Window span / prediction resolution 200 years
- Species/taxonomic group selection
  - two functional groups
  - two key dominant taxa
- Interactions estimated:
  - pine - oak (in this example)
:::
::::


## Lake Tulane results {.smaller}

![](./figures/tulane_B_C_ESA.svg){fig-align="center" fig-alt="Lake Tulane results: estimated driver effects (B matrix) and taxa interactions (C matrix), with climate covariates showing a stronger influence than the interactions. Pine-oak interactions are more strongly negative in the pine -> oak direction."}

::: {.callout-note  title="Supported hypotheses"}
 _Given the data_, taxa interactions are important but climate covariates have a stronger influence. Pine-oak interactions are more strongly negative in the pine -> oak direction.
:::

## Lake Tulane summary {.smaller}

This case study shows how **data** decisions are made to accommodate imperfect covariate data, and how **model** decisions are made to test hypotheses about climatic drivers and biotic interactions.


## Sunfish Pond variables {.smaller}

![](./figures/sunfish_palaeo_plot.png){fig-align="center" fig-alt="Sunfish Pond record: time series of the five dominant pollen taxa alongside the lake-level covariate over roughly 13,800 years"}

## Fitting Sunfish Pond {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
#### Questions/hypotheses:

- Are local climatic conditions (hydroclimate) a stronger driver of change than taxa interactions?
:::
::: {.column}
#### Parameter selection:

- Window span / prediction resolution 100 years
- Species/taxonomic group selection
  - five most dominant taxa
  - eastern white pine, hemlock, beech, oak, and birch
- Interactions estimated:
  - pine - oak - beech (in this example)
:::
::::


## Sunfish Pond results {.smaller}

![](./figures/sunfish_B_C_MEE.svg){fig-align="center" fig-alt="Sunfish Pond results: estimated driver effects (B matrix) and taxa interactions (C matrix), with lake level most important to hemlock and pine"}


::: {.callout-note  title="Supported hypotheses"}
Local conditions most important to hemlock and pine. Interactions are important to pine-beech and pine-oak dynamics. Nothing significant at 0.05, but we do not recommend relying on P-values to test hypotheses [@anderson2000].
:::


## Sunfish Pond summary {.smaller}

This case study demonstrates a partial interaction matrix, i.e., including bidirectional interactions of pine with other taxa but not including beech-oak interactions.

Because of the short time series we cannot estimate all interactions at once but we can make **model** decisions to break up the analyses into testable components. That is, build a more complete picture by pairwise model fits.


## Comparison of case studies {.smaller}

_`multinomialTS` does not show causality but lends statistical support to hypotheses._

<br>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
#### Lake Tulane

- Long record
- Multiple covariates
- Few dominant taxa
- Key drivers and interactions detected
- Likely a loss of fine-scale processes like post-fire recovery

:::
::: {.column}
#### Sunfish Pond

- Holocene record
- Single covariate
- Multiple key taxa
- Finer-scale resolution
- Likely to be missing explanatory power from unmeasured drivers
:::
::::

## In practice {.smaller}

Note that for both case studies, I only showed results for testing one hypothesis/component. The full analyses for both sites include multiple model fits to piece together the full story of ecosystem dynamics.


# Thank you for listening!


asenaq@caryinstitute.org

::: footer

![](./figures/color-UWcrest-print.png){width=55 fig-alt="University of Wisconsin–Madison crest"} ![](./figures/NSF_Official_logo_High_Res_1200ppi.png){width=90 fig-alt="U.S. National Science Foundation logo"}
:::

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
